sound_type: wing rustle on stream surface -- barely audible membranous wing flutter during emergence from aquatic nymphal stage onto bankside rocks
ecosystem: Cold stream. Clean, well-oxygenated mountain streams and rivers. Rocky substrate. Forested riparian zones.
season: Late spring through summer (emergence and hatch). Adults are short-lived (days to weeks).

coordinates.description: Reference for cold stream habitat in the Cascade foothills, Washington. Stonefly nymphs in clean mountain streams throughout the Cascades and Olympics.

taxonomy_note.description: PERLIDAE -- NEW FAMILY (44th). PLECOPTERA -- NEW ORDER (6th insect order). First stoneflies. First insect with an aquatic life stage. Stream indicator species. Presence indicates clean, cold, well-oxygenated water.
taxonomy_note.name_etymology: Perlidae from Latin perla ('pearl'), referring to the pale, somewhat pearlescent appearance of some species. Plecoptera from Greek plekein ('to braid, to fold') + pteron ('wing'), referring to the pleated hindwings.
taxonomy_note.genus_note: Family-level entry. PNW Perlidae include Calineuria californica, Hesperoperla pacifica, and other large predatory stoneflies common in Cascade and Olympic mountain streams.
taxonomy_note.order_note: Plecoptera -- NEW ORDER. Sixth insect order in the engine. Stoneflies. Approximately 3,500 species worldwide. Distinguished from all prior insect orders in the engine by having an obligate aquatic nymphal stage. Nymphs live in cold streams for 1-4 years before emerging as short-lived adults.
taxonomy_note.class_note: Insecta -- eight insect entries in degrees 87-94. The insect era extends. Six orders represented.
taxonomy_note.family_position: Perlidae enters as the 44th family at degree 94. NEW FAMILY. NEW ORDER. First stoneflies. First aquatic insect. Stream indicator.

song_metrics.fundamental_frequency_range_hz: Broad-spectrum, 500-8,000 Hz. The rustle occupies the same frequency range as stream babble and is often masked by it.
song_metrics.peak_energy_band_hz: No clear peak. Broad, low-amplitude rustling.
song_metrics.song_frequency_hz: NOT a song. NOT a call. PHYSICAL SOUND of wing membranes flexing and drying on rock and water surfaces during emergence.
song_metrics.song_duration_s: Individual emergence: minutes. The wing-drying and flexing process occurs over the first minutes after eclosion.
song_metrics.note_structure: No note structure. Continuous low-amplitude rustling. No rhythm, no pitch, no pattern -- the sound of thin membranes interacting with hard surfaces and moving water.
song_metrics.note_types: Wing rustle (emergence sound), abdominal drumming (male courtship signal transmitted through substrate, not airborne). The drumming is a vibrational signal, not an acoustic one -- it travels through rock, not air.
song_metrics.phrase_structure: No phrase structure. The wing rustle is a continuous, variable, low-amplitude physical sound.
song_metrics.timbre: Papery. Delicate. Membranous. Whispery. The thinnest, most delicate sound in the engine's insect repertoire. Wings like tissue paper flexing on wet rock.
song_metrics.amplitude: E=2. Barely audible. Individual emergence on a bankside rock. The sound is present but nearly lost in the stream's own acoustic environment. You hear it only when close and attentive.
song_metrics.context: EMERGENCE EVENT. Nymphs crawl from stream onto rocks. Adults emerge from nymphal exoskeleton. Wings dry and flex. The sound occurs during the vulnerable transition from aquatic to terrestrial. Not a communication signal -- a physical byproduct of emergence.
song_metrics.regional_variation: Multiple Perlidae species produce similar wing-rustling during emergence. Calineuria californica and Hesperoperla pacifica are the largest PNW species with the most audible emergence.
song_metrics.key_property: WING RUSTLE ON STREAM SURFACE -- the 95th acoustic element (species side). Barely audible membranous wing flutter during emergence. E=2. Quietest insect sound. Physical sound, not a signal. Stream indicator species -- presence of this sound confirms clean, cold, well-oxygenated water. Returns the engine to streams since degree 57. First aquatic insect.

physical_description.overall: Family-level entry. Adult stoneflies: 15-50 mm body length (Perlidae are among the largest). Elongated, flattened body. Two pairs of membranous wings held flat over the body at rest. Long cerci (tail filaments). Typically brown, tan, or golden coloration.
physical_description.diagnostic_mark: For degree 94: (1) ELONGATED FLAT BODY -- wider than tall, adapted from aquatic streamlining. (2) WINGS FLAT OVER BODY -- not tent-like, not folded; flat. (3) LONG CERCI -- two tail filaments visible at rear. (4) FOUND ON ROCKS NEAR STREAMS -- emergence habitat.
physical_description.skin_detail: Thin exoskeleton. Brown to golden coloration. Membranous wings with visible venation. Nymphs have external gills.
physical_description.dental_detail: Nymphs: predatory mandibles for capturing other aquatic invertebrates. Adults: reduced or absent mouthparts (many adult stoneflies do not feed).
physical_description.field_id: Elongated, flattened insects with wings held flat over the body, found on rocks near cold mountain streams. Long cerci (tail filaments). Brown or golden. Flight is weak and low. Often found resting on bankside rocks, bridges, and streamside vegetation during emergence season. Nymphs found under rocks in fast-flowing stream riffles -- their presence indicates clean water.

nodes.title: Wing Rustle on Stream Surface -- 95th Acoustic Element (species side)
nodes.description: Barely audible membranous wing flutter during emergence from aquatic nymphal stage. E=2. Quietest insect sound. Physical sound, not a signal. The sound of wings drying on wet rock. Returns the engine to streams.

nodes.title: Perlidae Enters -- 44th Family (NEW) / Plecoptera -- 6th Insect Order (NEW)
nodes.description: First stoneflies. First insect with an aquatic life stage. NEW FAMILY and NEW ORDER. Nymphs live 1-4 years in cold streams. Predatory aquatic nymphs. Six insect orders now in engine.

nodes.title: Stream Indicator Species -- Clean Cold Well-Oxygenated Water
nodes.description: The stonefly's presence IS the data. If stoneflies are present, the stream is healthy. If absent, something is wrong. Clean water, cold water, high dissolved oxygen, rocky substrate. The most reliable freshwater health indicator.

nodes.title: Return to Streams -- First Since Degree 57 (37-Degree Gap)
nodes.description: Belted Kingfisher (57) was the last stream species. 37-degree gap. The stonefly returns the engine to streams -- but from INSIDE the stream. Nymphs live IN the water. The first species to occupy the stream itself, not just use it from above.

nodes.title: First Aquatic Insect
nodes.description: All prior insects in the engine were terrestrial or aerial. The stonefly nymph is aquatic -- living under rocks in stream riffles for 1-4 years. The insect era enters the water. A new habitat category for Insecta.

nodes.title: The Erik Blood Contrast -- Producer's Palette vs Wing Rustle
nodes.description: Degree 94 pairs the stonefly with Erik Blood in Part A. Producer's palette (technology, studio, E=4) vs wing rustle (nature, stream, E=2). Both connect: Blood connects scenes through production; the stonefly connects stream health to insect presence. The cross-genre bridge and the stream indicator.

cross_references.relationship: STREAM SHARED! Both stream species. Kingfisher watches from above (degree 57); stonefly lives IN the stream (degree 94). First stream species since degree 57 -- 37-degree gap closed. The rattle announces; the rustle barely whispers.

STREAM CONNECTION. Dipper forages in fast mountain streams -- the same habitat where stonefly nymphs live. Predator-prey: dippers eat stonefly nymphs. Both stream health indicators. Both require clean, cold water.
SHARED E=2. Both E=2, both individual insect sounds. Mosquito whines in AIR; stonefly rustles on WATER surface. Aerial vs aquatic. The quietest insect pair in the engine.
INSECT ERA CONTEXT. Eight insect entries (87-94). But the stonefly breaks the terrestrial/aerial pattern -- first aquatic insect. The insect era enters the water.

OLD-GROWTH STREAM NEIGHBOR. Marten in old-growth forest near streams (65). Stonefly in the streams themselves (94). Both require intact forested riparian zones. Old-growth canopy shades streams, keeping them cold enough for stoneflies.
